channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Total consumer spending on games topped $60B in the US in 2025
item.content:encoded:
The total consumer spending on games topped $60 billion in the US in 2025, according to the latest report by the Entertainment Software Association (ESA).

As shown in the ESA's 2026 Essential Facts About the US Video Game Industry report, video games are outpacing consumer spending on movies, TV, and music combined.

Sourced via Circana, the total figure amounts to $60.7 billion. This is made out of $52.3 billion spent on content, $5.4 billion on hardware, and $2.95 billion on accessories. The figure is almost as high as 2021, the year with the highest spending thus far at $61.7 billion.

The report also includes a breakdown from a US-based survey to showcase more details about the total consumer spending. It's important to note that these surveys were conducted by YouGov in February of this year, with 13,545 total respondents.

The number of respondents includes 9,932 players who spend at least one hour per week playing video games on a smartphone, tablet, PC, console, or VR headset. Out of the 9,932, 7,971 are adults, and 1,961 are kids or teens. The remaining 3,613 are adults who either don't play video games or spend less than an hour per week playing.

Related:Team17 has laid off members of its marketing and communications department

As per the report, 43 percent of the surveyed consumers purchased a game. 33 percent were digital copies, and 24 percent were physical. It's worth noting that 58 percent of respondents opted for a free download. 35 percent purchased a game subscription, and 19 percent borrowed, traded, or rented a game.

What attributes are deemed most important when considering a new game? Among total players over the age of 18, it comes down to price (52 percent), quality of gameplay (47 percent), interesting story/premise (41 percent), the game's genre (41 percent), the quality of the graphics (37 percent), and whether or not it includes single player gameplay (37 percent).

When it comes to spending on cosmetic items and downloadable content, 58 percent of the surveyed players over the age of 8 have ever purchased in-game content. In decreasing order, this includes in-game currency (34 percent), skins or customization items (27 percent), season passes/battle passes (26 percent), expansion packs (26 percent), additional maps/levels (19 percent), and randomized content packs (15 percent).

The ESA report also has an interesting metric about parental direction when it comes to in-game purchases. 54 percent of parents purchased in-game content for their children. 93 Percent of parents who have purchased in-game content for their children said that they needed to approve purchases.

Related:Mina the Hollower sells 300,000 copies in three days

About the Author

Contributing Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Diego Nicolás Argüello is a freelance journalist and critic from Argentina. Video games helped him to learn English, so now he covers them for places like The New York Times, NPR, Rolling Stone, and more. He also runs Into the Spine, a site dedicated to fostering and supporting new writers, and co-hosted Turnabout Breakdown, a podcast about the Ace Attorney series. He’s most likely playing a rhythm game as you read this.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/total-consumer-spending-on-games-topped-60b-in-the-us-in-2025
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Team17 has laid off members of its marketing and communications department
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

June 3, 2026

1 Min Read

UK studio Team17 has made an undisclosed number of layoffs within its marketing and communications department. 

It's unclear how many people have been impacted, but at least two people made redundant by the Worms developer-publisher indicated the entire internal PR team has been jettisoned in posts on Bluesky and LinkedIn.

A statement provided to Game Developer by Team17 confirmed some roles have been eliminated.

"Following a review of our business operations, we have made changes to our marketing and communications structure. We would like to thank the colleagues affected for their contribution to Team17 and the support they have provided to our games, development partners and communities," said a spokesperson for the company. 

"While our structure has evolved, our commitment to supporting our games, development partners, players, media and creator communities remains unchanged. We continue to work closely with both internal teams and external partners to ensure our communications, creator engagement and media relations activities continue to support the success of our games and partners."

Related:Mina the Hollower sells 300,000 copies in three days

The company, which has also published notable titles such as Overcooked, Hell Let Loose, and Dredge, previously laid off a significant number of staff in 2023. 

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/team17-has-laid-off-members-of-its-marketing-and-communications-department
